sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 2 actions in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: LEFT, LEFT.
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 104, level 1.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, SPACE, MOUSE.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current knowledge ledger from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE LEDGER.
Pass it as the `world_model` argument of the `python` tool call, alongside `code`. That argument is the one that carries forward, so send it on every call, even when you reply with a tool call and no assistant text.
Game-tier fields (action_semantics, object_taxonomy, hud_map, win_pattern, level_log, cross_level_notes) persist across levels; level-tier fields (world_model, goal_model, recent_findings, open_questions, current_plan, strategy_log, failed_probes) reset when a level ends. Record every unproductive probe in failed_probes so it is never repeated, and tag claims VERIFIED(step N) or HYPOTHESIS.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Knowledge ledger carried from earlier turns:
Game knowledge (persists across levels):
- Action semantics: {'RIGHT': 'Moves character right by 3 cols', 'DOWN': 'Moves character down by 3 rows', 'LEFT': 'Moves character left by 3 cols', 'UP': 'Moves character up by 3 rows', 'SPACE': 'Rotates/transforms character'}
- Object taxonomy: {'white_shape': 'Player character (P-shape)', 'red_squares': '3x3 red blocks', 'green_frame': 'Green frame template (P-shape)', 'gray_blocks': 'Gray blocks from interactions'}
- HUD map: {'top_bar': 'charcoal bar at row 0'}
- Win pattern: Unknown - game over reached without completing level
- Level log: {'level_1': 'Game over at step 76, score 0. Did not complete level.'}
Current level:
- World model: Game over state. Character is white P-shape. Green frame is also P-shape. Red blocks are 3x3 squares. Gray blocks appear from interactions.
- Goal model: Fit character into green frame by rotating and moving
- Recent findings: ['Character shape changes with rotation (SPACE)', 'Character moves in 3-cell increments (UP/DOWN/LEFT/RIGHT)']
- Open questions: ['What exact configuration completes the level?']
- Plan: Game over - no more actions possible
- Strategy log: ['Cycling through orientations and moving character did not complete level - game over reached']
- Failed probes: ['Multiple SPACE rotations without completing level', 'Multiple UP/DOWN/LEFT/RIGHT moves without completing level']
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it. Tag claims VERIFIED(step N) when the evidence is in `history`, otherwise HYPOTHESIS.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
The `world_model` tool argument is the reliable way to update the ledger. Assistant text is optional; if you write any, keep it short and prefix lines with `Action semantics:`, `Object taxonomy:`, `HUD map:`, `Win pattern:`, `Level log:`, `Cross-level notes:`, `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, or `Failed probes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 103 actions; a strong score needs about 29 or fewer. You are 3.6x over the target.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Looking at the current image:
- The green frame is now at the top right
- The white character is below the green frame
- The red blocks are to the left of the white character
The positions have changed:
- Green: pixels=108, boundary: [[29, 41], [29, 49], [49, 49], [49, 47], [32, 47], [31, 46]]
  - Green frame: rows 29-49, cols 41-49
- White: pixels=135, boundary: [[41, 38], [41, 46], [55, 46], [55, 38], [53, 38], [52, 37]]
  - White character: rows 41-55, cols 38-46
So the green frame is at rows 29-49, cols 41-49, and the white character is at rows 41-55, cols 38-46.
The white character is below the green frame (rows 41-55 vs rows 29-49), and to the left (cols 38-46 vs cols 41-49).
Looking at the image, the green frame is a P-shape outline, and the white character is a solid P-shape. The white character appears to be below and to the left of the green frame.
I need to move the white character UP and RIGHT to align with the green frame.
But wait, looking at the image more carefully:
- The green frame is a P-shape outline at the top right
- The white character is a solid P-shape below the green frame
- The red blocks are to the left of the white character
The white character is below the green frame, so I need to move UP.
The white character is to the left of the green frame, so I need to move RIGHT.
Let me try moving UP and RIGHT to align with the green frame.
Actually, looking at the boundary data:
- Green: rows 29-49, cols 41-49
- White: rows 41-55, cols 38-46
The white character is at rows 41-55, and the green frame is at rows 29-49. So the white character is below the green frame (row 41 > row 29).
For columns:
- White: cols 38-46
- Green: cols 41-49
The white character is at cols 38-46, and the green frame is at cols 41-49. So the white character is to the left of the green frame (col 38 < col 41).
So I need to move UP and RIGHT to align with the green frame.
Let me try moving UP and RIGHT.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Check current positions
seg = current_frame.segmentation
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] == 'W' and n['pixels'] > 50:
        print(f"White: pixels={n['pixels']}, boundary: {n['boundary'][:6]}")
    if n['color'] == 'N' and n['pixels'] > 50:
        print(f"Green: pixels={n['pixels']}, boundary: {n['boundary'][:6]}")

# Green: rows 29-49, cols 41-49
# White: rows 41-55, cols 38-46
# White is below and left of green
# Need UP and RIGHT to align
# UP: (41-29)/3 = 4 moves
# RIGHT: (41-38)/3 = 1 move
action(['UP', 'UP', 'UP', 'UP', 'RIGHT'])
</parameter>
<parameter=world_model>
{"game_tier": {"action_semantics": {"RIGHT": "Moves character right by 3 cols", "DOWN": "Moves character down by 3 rows", "LEFT": "Moves character left by 3 cols", "UP": "Moves character up by 3 rows", "SPACE": "Rotates/transforms character"}, "object_taxonomy": {"white_shape": "Player character (P-shape)", "red_squares": "3x3 red blocks", "green_frame": "Green frame template (P-shape)", "gray_blocks": "Gray blocks from interactions"}, "hud_map": {"top_bar": "charcoal bar at row 0"}, "win_pattern": "Fit white P-shape into green P-frame by moving and rotating", "level_log": {"level_1": "Character rotated and moved. Need to align precisely with green frame."}, "cross_level_notes": {}}, "level_tier": {"world_model": "Green frame at rows 29-49, cols 41-49. White character at rows 41-55, cols 38-46. White is below and left of green.", "goal_model": "Move character UP 4, RIGHT 1 to align with green frame", "recent_findings": ["Green frame is larger than expected (108 pixels)", "Character orientation may need adjustment"], "open_questions": ["Will UP 4, RIGHT 1 align character with green frame?"], "current_plan": "Move UP 4, RIGHT 1 to align with green frame", "strategy_log": [], "failed_probes": []}}
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
